speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Steve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 17, 1995. Interviewing Jack Skovronski. Tape number four. When you got to Israel, were you then able to start to find out what was going on in the world? Or what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: General news. Nothing close from-- to my-- from my hometown. Just generally news. Read the paper, hear what the Jewish leader [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] intervened with the British and American governments to bomb the concentration camps. And it wasn't done. He went up on a building and threw himself down, committed suicide. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the name. Was the leader of the Jewish community in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], I just can't remember the name. But that's well-documented, so everybody knows that this happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other than that, no close news. Only from the newspapers. We read the newspaper, what-- what's going on. We know exactly what's going on in Poland. And the Germans, and Palestine itself, was very danger, because the Germans were in El Alamein, which was not far from Alexandria, Egypt. And it was close to Palestine. But they try not to [COUGHS] bring in panic to the population. The-- Tel Aviv was bombed twice by the Italians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not too big of damage, but it was bombed twice. And we knew about that, of course. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing in particular about whatever is going on. Because we knew exactly what's-- what's happening in Europe. And the Jewish leaders knew, too, that it happened, disaster.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- you alluded, various times, to survivorship. The-- like skills-- do you find-- was their skills or talent? What do you attribute your survivorship to?

speaker: interviewee
text: My survivorship I-- I attribute to pure luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many times I was in danger of-- one time I went to-- to look for a little food, and I went a-- in a-- just a dump. And I fell in, and I couldn't get out, because it was such a heavy ground and water that I couldn't get out. And fortunately, somebody went by and heard my cries and just helped me out of that water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So pure luck. Nothing else but pure luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want to say I'm smarter than the other guy which was bitten by a mosquito in Siberia, and he was a guy from Warsaw, my age. And from [INAUDIBLE] 18. And we knew that [INAUDIBLE] 18 was the Warsaw-- what-- the-- the Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that-- just can't find the proper expression. The headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Headquarters of the Jewish resistance movement. And he died from malaria. He was bitten by a mosquito that carried the-- the sickness of malaria, in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't want to say I'm a hero or I'm smarter than the other guy. It just pure luck. Meant to be. So I'm still around. How did you find your brother? Or how did he find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right after the war, there were all kind of agencies, Jewish agencies, trying to find one another. We had a lot of people that survived from our town, which knew him. And they were already in Palestine. They came to Palestine, one with the one girl, and they said, your brother survived, and he's in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked in Israel for a while in the-- it's called the NAAFI, Navy Army and Air Force Institute. There was a canteen, like, and there was soldier going to Germany. I wrote a letter, and he promised me that he's going to deliver that letter. I-- I knew already the address where he is, and he promised me that he-- and he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother wrote me a letter back. I gave him the address, of course, in-- in Palestine. And he gave me-- he write me a letter. And from then on, we were in contact. He came, I think, to this country '49-- '49, I think. Yeah, around that time. And he went to Springfield, Illinois, and that's where he settled down and he made his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to this country in '57, did-- did people ask you about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't publicized like it is today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We weren't-- a lot of people-- I'm a big talker, a big-- trying to say what I went through. I'm not a hero, I don't want to be a hero what I went through it. Millions of people went through, and worse circumstances and conditions. But we didn't talk at the beginning a lot. But lately when the-- it's more publicized. They even teach it in schools, the Holocaust. So I have my part of story to tell. Whatever happened, you know. But I'm not attributing to big talent. Or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just sheer luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- did your experiences throughout all of this, did this affect how you-- how you raised your children or created a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- I got married in Israel in '47, March '47. And I joined the Israeli army. I was fighting on all fronts of Israel. Very active. I was a sergeant in the [HEBREW], which is artillery, [HEBREW]. That's the name of the-- where I belong. And I was very active in very dangerous times, too. I went through-- one time Egyptians bombarded our place, and-- and I had a little, small injury on my leg. Very, very small. On-- just a little bleeding, while the guy next to me got killed. That was really a shock. That was really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first time I saw-- you know, it just shocked me. I was in a shock for a couple of days. I couldn't get over it. But that subsides after a while, and get used to it. And then year after-- later on, in the other wars, which are, like, '56, and I still was in the '56 in the deserts of-- after Gaza-- and the deserts are really by the Egyptian front. And in the reserves, because after they liberate-- they send us home after-- also approximately-- everything goes in 14 months and 15 months there was in the Israeli army. My daughter was born in the end of '47, so while I was in the army, we-- I had my wife worked in a store, in a shoe store. And we lived with our-- with her parents. But they came to-- to Palestine in 1933, way before the war. So I lived with them, and we worked and we tried to build our own nest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- I worked in a bakery, delivered bread through this. And we bought a condo in Ramat Gan. Brand new everything. It was like a paradise, it was like a palace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we had friends. We got ahead, you know. It was-- you're young, and that's what you're aiming to try to better yourself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And in '57 I left Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I came to United States. It wasn't easy, either. Was just as hard as the beginning is, the settlement in Israel. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you try to forget. You try to keep on going and doing the best you can. And was fairly successful in life, the years in America. After a couple of years, open up a store, men's furnishing, men's and boys. I was doing all right. Fairly good, made a living. Educated our children. My daughter has a master degree in teaching, and my other one is a lawyer. And she's practicing law in San Francisco, which we're very proud of both of them. This one is married, the other one is not. We have two grandchildren, which are right in front of you here. And we love them dearly. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything? Well, is there anything-- anything we didn't-- we didn't talk about that you would want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we covered the fair territory of-- of all the things that I went through. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's nothing in particular to mention in Israel, Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Persia and Russia, we covered everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would tell, say, your grandchildren or your great-great grandchildren about your experiences? About the Holocaust and your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson, when he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when he was 16, he wrote-- he's a very talented boy-- and he wrote an essay, How the State of Israel Was Born. I have it, if you want to take a look at it. I can even show it to you. And he's very-- wanted to know a lot. So he interviewed me. I don't even know where the tape is or  he made it-- if we made a tape of it. This is more formal than-- than the-- the other one. And I leave them to them, the tape, so once in a while they'll remind themselves of their grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then play the tape, and see what-- what life is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not always pinky, you know? But we went exceptionally through a hard time toward.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guys that were born my age, you know, because younger ones, they took to the oven. And older ones were hard to survive. But I was just born in 1920, and 19 years old when the war broke out. So the-- the age was in favor of me, just to survive better than-- than-- than others. And I didn't go to the concentration camps in Germany like my brother did, because he tells horrible story. My sister-in-law, which is also-- was also a survive-- they're both gone, may they rest in peace-- the sister-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a German-- she had a son with him which was 15 years old, which survived the camp-- the camps. So she had exceptionally hard in the concentration camp, to think about herself and about her son. He survived. He died of cancer just eight, ten years ago. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she tells horrible stories what they went through. So my story's a paradise in comparison. They went through-- my sister-in-law tells me that they one time-- they caught her stealing a piece of bread. So one gentlemen, SS man, had a board with nails and told her to take off the dress. And he hit that about 10, 15 times in the back, which she had holes in her body. Then she passed away. Visible, visible-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] visible [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] damage. Physical, visible damage to her body.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went through much more than, and she survived and saving her son. And her son had a grandfather in-- in America. So right after the war, what they found out that their grandpa-- so he brought his grandson down here. And he was very young. He was 13 when the war broke out. So he joined in the American army. And he came back to Germany, and they were still in camp. Their parents were still in camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they went through much more than I-- than I have to. But it's-- I think, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't go through that now, because I'm too old for that. But when you're young, you fight. You fight. You struggle. You fight for existence, for life. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when you're ready. OK. Any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife Sarah, to my right, of 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it, hold it, hold it. Hold it. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right is my lovely wife Sarah, of 48 years. And out of that life-- love-- came two wonderful daughters. My left is Jona, and my younger daughter, Nikki, is in San Francisco. She's out of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else? Do you have questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy's going to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family went to Palestine, my grandmother with child-- with her family went in 1920 to Palestine. My father left in beginning of 1913, and he was 18 months alone. And we came in 1933 to Palestine. Then the war broke out, and my husband came in 1942 to Palestine. I was 16 when I met him, but we didn't have nothing to do with each other. He left for Eritrea for two-- a-- two years. And he came in '44. In '46 we met really. That was-- at that time, I went to introduce him to my cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- we started to date. In '47, we married. And we have our children. But he never really, in the beginning, talked about anything. Of course, we didn't know-- knew what happened when he came in '42. It wasn't really clear what's happening in Europe exactly with the concentration camps. He thought he had a hard time, but then, in '45, when everybody started to come, as he saw, he had it very hard, but not what has happened there. And in '57, we came here to his brother. Life was ups and down. Hard and better. And here we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Israel in 1947. I was 10 years old when we came to this country. It was a difficult-- a difficult adjustment, a difficult beginning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It-- it was unique to be brought up in a household where they-- where people-- where the parents were foreign and not American. Although my parents adjusted beautifully. They spoke English immediately. They considered their children's education very important. And both my sister and I received-- we got our college degrees. My sister is a lawyer in San Francisco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was interesting. My father did not speak about his experiences in Europe very freely. He was bottled up. Most of the information I got about the Holocaust, about Europe, about what happened there, was from his friends and from his-- and relatives. I had an aunt that was the exact opposite of him. She spoke about daily. She lived it. She re-lived it daily. He did not. He was-- he was more bottled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his-- he communicates a little differently, which is why we decided we would do this in order to record and share his experiences for my children, who know a little bit more than I because he did share more as he got older. And he had grandchildren, he wanted more and more for them to know his experiences. So here we are. 75 years old. Happy birthday. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the book that the survivors of our town edited and issued it in Israel. I got a copy of it from-- from the management that were involved in that book.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the monument for all the people of our little town. The name and all the people that were burned in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the synagogue that the Germans burned. I may have mentioned it in the interview that the Germans burned down the synagogue, and they said the Jews-- forced to sign-- that they did the-- the burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, the whole committee of the bank that I also mentioned in the interview. Here he is sitting in the front row with the rest of the people that were involved in that bank with no profits.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me when I came to Palestine and took the first photo in 1942. I was 22 years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. Married in March 25, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my lovely wife Sarah. And survivors of the Holocaust that survived in Israel at my wedding party.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that I got left from my hometown. My sister-- where I point my finger at, here. Right here-- with her boyfriend [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the war, before the tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely family at bat mitzvah of my granddaughter. My daughter, my granddaughter, my son-in-law, my older daughter, my grandson, my wife, and I.
